text: We run an active bug bounty program (e.g., HackerOne/Bugcrowd) since [date] with a defined scope and payouts. We publish metrics such as number of reports, median triage time, and time-to-fix by severity.
Customer penetration testing and red teaming are allowed with a clear approval process, scope definition, and safeguards. Third-party testers are permitted under agreed rules of engagement.
We maintain a public vulnerability disclosure policy (VDP) outlining submission channels, triage SLAs, coordinated disclosure timelines, and communication practices. security.txt is published.
We track and disclose remediation SLAs by severity (e.g., critical within X days). We can discuss past critical vulnerabilities, root causes, and fixes, demonstrating continuous improvement.
We have a limited or invite-only bug bounty program with restricted scope and minimal public metrics.
Customer testing may be allowed in certain environments or windows, but the process and scope are not clearly documented.
We provide a vulnerability reporting channel (e.g., email), but lack defined SLAs or detailed disclosure procedures.
We handle vulnerabilities internally, but do not share metrics such as time-to-fix or historical data.
Customer penetration testing or red teaming is not allowed.
We do not run a bug bounty program or engage with external researchers.
We may take legal action against unauthorized security research.
We do not have a formal vulnerability disclosure policy or process.
